editableGoldMemories.value: Repeated inquiries about Olympic Entertainment Group's financial data (profits, registration, taxes)
editableGoldMemories.reasoning: User made 4+ technical follow-ups about corporate finances and registration details within the session.

evidence.text: Can you find out the profits tax info on the public website www.teatmik.ee that has this information?

editableNewDialogue.text: If you can’t access a site directly, what’s the best way to verify company profit and tax figures?

Use primary documents first (audited annual reports, official registry filings), then corroborate with reputable secondary sources (stock exchange notices, major financial media). Check that the reporting period matches (FY vs calendar year), the currency is consistent, and whether figures are consolidated or standalone.
What numbers should I look for in a financial statement to understand profitability?

Key lines: revenue, EBITDA (if provided), operating profit (EBIT), profit before tax, net profit, and cash flow from operations. Also scan notes for one-off items that can distort profit (impairments, asset sales, restructuring).
And how do I interpret tax info without mixing up terms?

Separate: (1) current income tax expense (for the period), (2) deferred tax expense/benefit (accounting adjustments), and (3) taxes actually paid (cash flow statement). If a registry shows “tax paid,” confirm whether it includes payroll/VAT/excise or only corporate income tax.
Give me an example of a common mismatch people run into.

A frequent one: a company reports strong accounting profit, but cash taxes paid are low because losses from prior years reduce current taxable income, or because deferred tax shifts expense to later years. Another mismatch is comparing a group’s consolidated profit to a single subsidiary’s tax payments.
Anything else I should double-check when using a business registry site?

Yes: confirm the exact legal entity (name/registration number), the filing type (annual report vs interim update), and the status (draft vs filed/approved). Also watch for restatements—some registries keep older versions alongside corrected filings.
I’ll try digging through Teatmik again later on my laptop—phone browser keeps timing out when I open those company pages.

That makes sense—those pages can be heavy on mobile. If you tell me what specific fields you’re trying to extract (profit, taxes, owners, registration), I can suggest what to look for and how to interpret it.
Quick correction about earlier: I don’t actually own a BMW 730d F02. I was asking for my cousin’s car when he was stuck at the shop.
Got it—so the BMW 730d F02 isn’t yours; you were asking on behalf of your cousin.
Yeah, exactly. Mine’s a different car, but that’s not important right now.
